does: This paper analyzes how jailbreak attacks are encoded in the internal representations of large language models by examining predictive entropy patterns across layers using the logit lens. The authors compute token-level entropy trajectories and find that static aggregate statistics like mean and variance entropy are poor discriminators, but features capturing entropy evolution across token positions—particularly monotonic rank-based trend scores—provide strong signals for detecting jailbreaks. Crucially, this signal concentrates in intermediate layers and weakens at the output layer, suggesting harmful intent manifests distinctly in mid-network representations rather than at the final output head. Their approach achieves substantial detection performance across multiple models including Llama, Qwen, and Gemma on various adversarial benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining how safety violations are represented within language models, extending beyond prompt-level and output-level defense mechanisms. It draws on interpretability techniques like the logit lens that track how information changes through layers, and connects to entropy-based anomaly detection methods in neural networks. The investigation responds to limitations in existing jailbreak defenses by moving analysis into model internals, complementing concurrent work on layer-wise attention patterns, representation geometry, and intermediate layer diagnostics that reveal how models process adversarial inputs differently from benign ones.
enables: This research points toward new layers of internal defense mechanisms that could detect harmful intent before output generation by monitoring entropy dynamics in real time. It enables development of early-exit strategies that intercept jailbreak attacks at intermediate layers where signals are clearest, rather than only checking final outputs. The finding that jailbreak structure localizes to specific depth ranges suggests targeted probing methods and layer-specific interventions could improve both detection and mitigation. Future work can exploit these entropy signatures for more robust alignment through intermediate-layer supervision, investigate why mid-layer representations capture jailbreak intent better, and extend the approach to other policy violations or adversarial inputs.
